headline: Shohei Ohtani Goes First Overall to North Correa, and Abbotsford-Colby Twins Stealing George Springer at Pick 53 Was the Draft's Best Move
subheadline: From Ohtani at #1 to Blake Snell in round 13, the 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft had everything — genius steals, baffling reaches, and one team that might have just built a dynasty.
body:
# 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft Review
### *By Bill Simmons, The Ringer*

---

## **I. The War Room Opens**

You know that scene in *Moneyball* where Billy Beane is pacing around the draft room, phone wedged between his ear and shoulder, making moves nobody else sees coming while everyone else is just following the board? That's every fantasy draft, except instead of scouts arguing about a guy's "good face," it's fourteen grown adults arguing about whether Blake Snell in round 13 is a genius move or a desperate cry for help. (Spoiler: it's the latter.) The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft went down on March 22nd, and I'm here to tell you it delivered — steals in the double digits, reaches that made me physically wince, and at least two managers who I'm convinced were drafting with one eye on a different screen entirely.

The headline steals are real: **North Correa** grabbing **Mookie Betts** at pick 28 (ADP 39), then following with **Chris Sale** at pick 29 (ADP 39) — back-to-back punches that should be illegal — and **Albuquerque Bombers** hoovering up **Sandy Alcantara**, **Zack Wheeler**, and **Carlos Rodón** like they were clearance-rack pitching. Meanwhile, the Canberra Wombats apparently decided that "reach" was just a concept for lesser managers and spent the draft proving them wrong in the most painful way possible. We'll get there.

But first, let's talk about the top of the draft, where the decisions were mostly fine and boring in the way that good decisions usually are — until they weren't. Pick seven and eight went to starting pitchers, which in a 12-category head-to-head league with QS scoring is either visionary or reckless depending on how your pitching holds up in May. This draft had everything. It had a defending champion (**Sugar Land Skeeters**) going semi-rogue with reaches. It had a two-time bottom-dweller (**General Hospital**) actually drafting competently for once. And it had **Buttville Skidmarks** — a name I will never not enjoy typing — quietly building something interesting in the middle rounds. Buckle up.

---

## **II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

**North Correa** held the first overall pick and did what any sane human being with a pulse does: they took **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** — the most dominant fantasy asset since the concept of "fantasy asset" was invented. In a league that scores OBP, HR, RBI, R, and SB, Ohtani as a pure batter in the Util slot is an absolute monster, and North Correa wisely sidestepped the pitcher version entirely (we'll get to who grabbed that). **Athletic Park Dragons** took **Aaron Judge** at two, which is correct. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** snagged **Juan Soto** at three, also correct. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** grabbed **José Ramírez** at four — a cornerstone pick for the reigning 2024 champion who clearly wants to run it back. **BaseballPro77** (a two-time champion, by the way, respect the résumé) took **Bobby Witt Jr.** at five, **Albuquerque Bombers** got **Ronald Acuña Jr.** back at six after what I assume was a very anxious few minutes, and then picks seven and eight went SP — **Hiroshima Radiation** with **Tarik Skubal** and **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** with **Paul Skenes**, both legitimate aces in a league that rewards strikeouts and QS.

Picks nine through fourteen are where it gets interesting. **Buttville Skidmarks** landed **Elly De La Cruz** at nine — elite SB upside, massive HR ceiling, the kind of pick that wins categories by itself. **General Hospital** took **Kyle Tucker** at ten, which is a very solid floor pick for a team that's finished 13th back-to-back. **Sugar Land Skeeters** grabbed **Garrett Crochet** at eleven — defending champs going pitching early, fine. **Riverside Roughnecks** took **Jackson Chourio** at twelve (breakout candidate, love it), **Canberra Wombats** got **Gunnar Henderson** at thirteen (borderline top-ten talent falling to them, nice), and **Benchwarmers** closed round one with **Julio Rodríguez** at fourteen. Overall? A clean, defensible first round with no truly catastrophic decisions. The chaos was coming.

---

## **III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**North Correa** — *Pick #1*
Starting from last (14th in 2024, 12th in 2025), North Correa came in swinging. **Ohtani (Batter)** at one is automatic. Then — and this is where it gets fun — they doubled up on **Mookie Betts** (pick 28, ADP 39) and **Chris Sale** (pick 29, ADP 39) in back-to-back turns like some kind of snake-draft cheat code. Sale was a Cy Young winner in 2025 and still has elite strikeout stuff; Betts is Betts. **Jack Flaherty** in round 10 at ADP 170 is another legitimate steal for a rotation that's quietly deep. The only blemish is **Jeff Hoffman** in round 13, taken at pick 169 when his ADP was 148 — a reach for a reliever who profiles as a volatile NSVH contributor. Still, this draft has the energy of a team that's done suffering. Two consecutive bottom-four finishes have a way of clarifying your priorities.
**Grade: A-** — *The back-to-back steals in rounds 2–3 alone justify the grade; the upside here is genuinely top-three in the league.*

---

**Athletic Park Dragons** — *Pick #2*
Coming off a brutal fall from 6th to 14th, the Dragons needed a statement draft. **Judge** at two is the foundation. **Cristopher Sánchez** at pick 27 is solid value — quietly one of the better contact suppressors in baseball. The late-round steals are real: **Willson Contreras** (pick 114, ADP 131), **Dansby Swanson** (pick 142, ADP 160), and **Gleyber Torres** (pick 167, ADP 180) give them unexpected depth at cheap prices. The middle of the draft — **Jhoan Duran**, **Abner Uribe** — shows a reliever-stacking tendency that can juice NSVH. **James Wood** in round 3 is a high-upside OF gamble. This roster has legitimate ceiling.
**Grade: B+** — *Judge plus three late-round steals plus reliever depth is a real formula; don't sleep on these guys bouncing back.*

---

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** — *Pick #3*
**Juan Soto** at three is perfect. **Bryan Woo** in round two is a smart SP investment. Then they grab **Trea Turner** in round three and **Mason Miller** in round four — a closer with filthy stuff who anchors the NSVH category. **Andrés Muñoz** in round six adds another elite arm. The **Brandon Woodruff** steal in round 9 (ADP 133) is great IF he's healthy, which is always the asterisk with Woody. **Taylor Ward** at pick 143 (ADP 128) is a mild reach for a guy who's been inconsistent, and **Matthew Boyd** in round 13 at ADP 194 is a low-risk high-reward dart. First season in the league, only a 10th-place finish to reference, but this draft looks like a team that knows what it's doing.
**Grade: B+** — *Soto, Turner, and a bullpen built for category domination — the Reapers are coming.*

---

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins** — *Pick #4*
The 2024 champions are not messing around. **José Ramírez** at four anchors everything — elite across R, HR, RBI, SB, and OBP. **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** in round two gives them another SB/power threat. **Yordan Alvarez** in round three is filthy value at that spot. **George Springer** in round four at pick 53 (ADP 89) is a STEAL — still a plus contributor who went 36 picks early. **Brandon Nimmo** in round nine (ADP 154) is another bargain. The one dark spot: **Blake Snell** in round 13 at pick 172 (ADP 98). Snell at ADP 98 taken at 172 sounds like a steal until you remember that Snell is a walking health disclaimer. They're reaching faith-based here, and that's a 74-pick gap of pure prayer.
**Grade: A-** — *The defending champ loaded up on upside while swiping two undervalued veterans; Snell is the wildcard that could make this an A or a B.*

---

**BaseballPro77** — *Pick #5*
Two-time champion, consistent top-three finisher, and they drafted exactly the way you'd expect a two-time champion to draft: boring, disciplined, and deep in starting pitching. **Bobby Witt Jr.** at five is a perfect pick. **Ketel Marte** in round two, **Logan Gilbert** in round three, **George Kirby** in round four — that's a rotation so deep it's almost aggressive. **Luis Castillo** in round seven (ADP 118) is a legitimately great steal for an ace who punches out batters in bunches. **Mike Trout** in round 13 is the high-risk pick of the draft — if he's healthy, genius; if he plays 40 games again, ouch. **Giancarlo Stanton** in round 14 has the same energy.
**Grade: B+** — *Methodical, rotation-heavy, steal-laden — this is what sustained success looks like, even if the lineup has some injury landmines.*

---

**Albuquerque Bombers** — *Pick #6*
Oh, the Bombers. Coming off a 3rd-place finish, they are absolutely *cooking* in the middle rounds. **Acuña Jr.** at six, **Francisco Lindor** at pick 23, **Freddie Freeman** in round four — that's a lineup. Then they go and pull **Christian Yelich** (ADP 102, taken at pick 79), **Zack Wheeler** (ADP 139, taken at pick 107), **Sandy Alcantara** (ADP 156, taken at pick 118), and **Carlos Rodón** (ADP 186, taken at pick 163). That's four legitimate steals scattered through the draft. The **Jorge Polanco** reach at pick 202 (ADP 191) is barely worth mentioning — it's round 15, who cares. **Brice Turang** in round three is an interesting upside play with SB potential.
**Grade: A** — *Best value across rounds 6–12 in the entire draft; if Alcantara and Wheeler are healthy, this rotation is elite.*

---

**Hiroshima Radiation** — *Pick #7*
Here's a fascinating quirk: in round eight, the Radiation grabbed **Shohei Ohtani (Pitcher)** at pick 106. So North Correa has the batter, and Hiroshima has the pitcher — the universe is balanced. **Tarik Skubal** leads the way at pick seven (justified, legitimate ace), and pairing him with **Ohtani the Pitcher** gives them a rotation with generational upside. **Bryce Harper** in round three is always a threat. **Roman Anthony** in round four is a high-ceiling prospect play. **Kyle Schwarber** in round two gives them HR pop for the power categories. **Nathan Eovaldi** in round nine (ADP 135) is a steal. The issue is this roster swings wildly — their historical finishes (2nd, 11th, 5th) suggest a team that can contend but also completely faceplant.
**Grade: B+** — *Two elite aces plus Schwarber/Harper in the lineup; the variance here is enormous, which is very on-brand for Hiroshima.*

---

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — *Pick #8*
Back-to-back top-five finishes (4th, 5th, 2nd) — the Wonderdogs are a model of consistency. **Paul Skenes** at eight is a perfect fit for a team that builds around pitching. **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** in round two gives them another top-tier arm. Then the big elephant in the room: **Pete Alonso** in round three at pick 36 (ADP 22). That's a 14-pick reach for a guy who had a down 2025 — I respect the loyalty, I do not respect the ADP violation. **Josh Hader** in round ten (ADP 121, taken at 133) is the second-worst value pick here. **Framber Valdez** in round six is quietly great. **Michael Harris II** and **Ozzie Albies** give them OF/2B depth.
**Grade: B** — *The Skenes/Yamamoto combo could be the best 1-2 pitching punch in the league; the Alonso reach hurts, but this team knows how to compete.*

---

**Buttville Skidmarks** — *Pick #9*
Look, I'm not here to judge the name. I'm here to tell you that the Skidmarks quietly drafted a fascinating roster. **Elly De La Cruz** at nine is elite — the SB upside alone makes this pick worth celebrating. **Junior Caminero** in round two is a high-ceiling 3B. **Wyatt Langford** in round three, **Rafael Devers** in round four — the lineup has teeth. **MacKenzie Gore** in round ten (ADP 149, taken at 132 — wait, that's a steal, taken 17 picks early) and **Bryan Reynolds** in round twelve (ADP 181, taken at 160) give them depth. **Spencer Strider** in round 11 is a massive upside play if he's fully back from Tommy John. The trajectory is steady improvement (11th, 8th, 8th) — this might be the year they break through.
**Grade: B+** — *De La Cruz plus Strider plus depth steals; if the Skidmarks name doesn't scare opponents, the roster might.*

---

**General Hospital** — *Pick #10*
Two consecutive 13th-place finishes. The pressure was real. And honestly? They answered. **Kyle Tucker** at ten is excellent — elite OBP, power, and runs. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** in round two, **Manny Machado** in round three — suddenly this lineup looks like a real thing. **Max Fried** in round four gives them an ace. **Luis Robert Jr.** in round eight (ADP 149, taken at 103 — that's a 46-pick steal) is the single most aggressive value play in the draft, banking on a bounce-back from a guy with 40-HR upside. **Corey Seager** in round six adds another quality bat. The concern is rotation depth beyond Fried, but **Eury Pérez**, **Cam Schlittler**, and **Bubba Chandler** are all interesting.
**Grade: B+** — *Finally, a draft that matches the ambition; if Robert bounces back, this is a top-five team.*

---

**Sugar Land Skeeters** — *Pick #11*
Defending champions. And they... reached. A lot. **Garrett Crochet** at eleven is the right pick — one of the best arms in baseball coming off a breakout. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** in round two is great. **Hunter Brown** in round three, fine. Then it starts getting weird: **Matt Olson** in round four (ADP 34, taken at pick 46 — 12-pick reach), **Edwin Díaz** in round six (ADP 54, taken at pick 74 — 20-pick reach for a closer coming off injury concerns), and **Ryan Helsley** in round eleven (ADP 137, taken at pick 151 — 14-pick reach). The saving grace is the late-round genius: **Max Muncy** in round twelve (ADP 188, taken at 158 — steal) and **Trent Grisham** in round fourteen (ADP 204, taken at 186 — steal). Defending champs with some reach anxiety. Relatable.
**Grade: B-** — *The top of the draft is great, the middle cost them equity they can't afford to give away as defending champs.*

---

**Riverside Roughnecks** — *Pick #12*
Three straight 9th-11th finishes — the definition of mediocre in a 14-team league. The Roughnecks needed something different, and rounds 3–7 delivered. **Cole Ragans** in round three (ADP 54, taken at pick 40 — 14-pick steal) is the best pure steal of rounds 1–4. **Jackson Chourio** at twelve anchors things. **Jackson Merrill** in round four is a rising star. **Trevor Story** in round seven (ADP 121, taken at pick 96 — steal) is a nice value play if healthy. **Jacob Misiorowski** in round eight is a filthy arm with upside. The catcher situation (**Shea Langeliers**, **Hunter Goodman**) is redundant but deep. **Carlos Estévez** (reach at pick 180, ADP 129) and **Kenley Jansen** (reach at pick 185, ADP 163) at the back end reflect an over-investment in aging/volatile closers.
**Grade: B-** — *Ragans steal and Chourio upside give this team a ceiling; the late-round closer hoarding is puzzling.*

---

**Canberra Wombats** — *Pick #13*
I say this with genuine affection: the Wombats drafted like someone who spent their prep time exclusively watching highlight reels from 2021. **Gunnar Henderson** at thirteen is lovely — great value, great player. **Cal Raleigh** in round two is the best fantasy catcher available, smart. **Logan Webb** and **Pete Crow-Armstrong** in rounds three and four, solid. Then. **Jacob deGrom** in round five (ADP 56, taken at pick 69 — 13-pick reach for a guy with approximately nine functioning vertebrae). **Jose Altuve** in round nine (ADP 98, taken at pick 125 — 27-pick reach). **Jo Adell** in round twelve (ADP 130, taken at pick 156 — 26-pick reach). **Aaron Nola** in round fifteen (ADP 192, taken at pick 209 — fine, whatever, it's round 15). This is a pattern, not a coincidence.
**Grade: C+** — *Great top-four picks followed by a reach festival that would embarrass a first-year manager; history of inconsistency (3rd, 12th, 4th) suggests this team will either contend or crater.*

---

**Benchwarmers** — *Pick #14*
The Benchwarmers had picks 14 and 15 — the classic "last pick, first pick of round 2" double-dip — and used it on **Julio Rodríguez** and **Corbin Carroll** back-to-back. That's two elite OF with speed/power profiles. Smart. **Zach Neto** and **Brent Rooker** in rounds three and four give them SS eligibility and OF power depth. **Cody Bellinger** in round five (ADP 57, taken at pick 70 — 13-pick reach) is the questionable call — Bellinger was a 2025 disappointment in a Yankees lineup that never maximized him. **Michael King** in round eleven is a legitimate SP. The back end gets thin fast.
**Grade: B-** — *The JRod/Carroll combo at the turn is genuinely exciting; the reach on Bellinger and thin late-round rotation depth keeps this from being more.*

---

## **IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**Three Best Steals:**

🏆 **Luis Robert Jr., Round 8, Pick 103 — General Hospital** (ADP 149, saved 46 picks): A 46-pick discount on a guy with legitimate 40-HR, 30-SB upside in a Mets lineup? In a category-scoring league? This is the steal of the entire draft. If Robert stays healthy and recaptures his 2023 form, General Hospital just won the draft in round eight.

🏆 **Cole Ragans, Round 3, Pick 40 — Riverside Roughnecks** (ADP 54, saved 14 picks): A legitimate frontline SP with triple-digit K upside going in round three when the board had him in round four? In a QS/K/ERA/WHIP league, that's free money.

🏆 **George Springer, Round 4, Pick 53 — Abbotsford-Colby Twins** (ADP 89, saved 36 picks): A 36-pick discount on a 30-HR threat who still runs and scores runs? The defending champs found free upside when nobody else was looking.

**Three Worst Reaches:**

💀 **Blake Snell, Round 13, Pick 172 — Abbotsford-Colby Twins** (ADP 98, reached 74 picks): I almost respect the audacity. You can't reach 74 picks in round 13 on a guy whose career has been defined by brilliance and inexplicable fragility. This is the draft equivalent of falling in love at the casino.

💀 **Edwin Díaz, Round 6, Pick 74 — Sugar Land Skeeters** (ADP 54, reached 20 picks): Defending champs spending a 20-pick premium on a closer coming off an injury-riddled 2025 campaign. Closers are volatile assets and the Skeeters burned real draft equity here.

💀 **Jacob deGrom, Round 5, Pick 69 — Canberra Wombats** (ADP 56, reached 13 picks): It's not just the ADP violation — it's the guy. Jacob deGrom in 2026 is essentially a fantasy lottery ticket with a very expensive price tag. Hope is a strategy, but it's not a *good* strategy.

---

## **V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

1. **General Hospital finishes top three.** I know, I know — two consecutive 13th-place finishes. But the **Luis Robert Jr.** steal in round eight combined with the **Guerrero/Tucker/Machado** lineup core is genuinely elite. This is the year they stop being the punchline.

2. **Canberra Wombats' deGrom pick ends in disaster.** He'll either miss 80 games or look like 2018 deGrom for six starts and then evaporate. The Wombats finish between 8th and 12th, right where their recent history suggests they belong.

3. **Albuquerque Bombers lead the league in ERA through the first half.** The Alcantara/Wheeler/Rodón steal trifecta gives them a rotation that — if healthy — is the best in the league. Three legitimate aces drafted at a discount doesn't happen often.

4. **Buttville Skidmarks breaks into the top five for the first time.** The De La Cruz/Strider/Devers core has the variance to make a run, and their steady improvement curve (11th → 8th → 8th) suggests they're about one good draft from breaking through. This might have been that draft.

5. **North Correa's back-to-back steals in rounds 2–3 become the most-talked-about draft moment of the season.** The Betts/Sale double-dip at the exact same ADP in consecutive picks is the kind of thing that ends up in a league's lore forever. If North Correa goes from 12th to a top-five finish, every league member will remember where they were sitting when it happened.

---

## **VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

My 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball champion is **Albuquerque Bombers**.

Here's the case: They enter with the third-best recent trajectory in the league (5th, 3rd, top-three energy), and they drafted a roster that covers every single category with elite depth. **Acuña Jr.** gives them a potential 40/40 OF anchor. **Lindor** runs the shortstop position. **Freeman** and **Yelich** fill the lineup with OBP and power. Then the pitching: **Wheeler**, **Alcantara**, and **Rodón** — three legitimate starters taken at a combined discount of 90+ ADP spots. That's not luck, that's drafting philosophy, and it's the Bombers' historical calling card (Yelich, Wheeler, Alcantara, Rodón all as steals — this is a pattern of behavior, not a coincidence).

The category profile is almost perfectly balanced: the lineup produces runs and OBP, the rotation covers K, QS, ERA, and WHIP, and there's enough positional depth to weather injuries in May and June. The only genuine weakness is catcher (Ivan Herrera is a backup-level contributor), but in a 14-team league, that's a manageable hole.

**BaseballPro77** is my pick for the team most likely to make it interesting in September — they always do, and the rotation is legitimately deep. But the Bombers have the kind of draft that looks better the more you stare at it, like one of those Magic Eye posters where suddenly you see the sailboat and can't believe you missed it.

Albuquerque won't just compete in 2026. They'll dominate. And when they hoist whatever digital trophy this league gives out — probably a MillerLite® can with a gold star on it — remember you read it here first.

*Now if you'll excuse me, I need to go explain to someone why drafting Blake Snell in round 13 is not, in fact, "value."*
